Course Title: AI in Industrial Control Systems: Hidden Risks
Category: Manufacturing: Chemicals, Food Manufacturing, Industrial Manufacturing, Textiles, Automotive, Pharmaceuticals, Electronics
Description: New vulnerabilities introduced in PLCs, SCADA, and control layers.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with industrial control systems or manufacturing environments is helpful, though no specialist technical knowledge is required to follow this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the new vulnerabilities that AI introduces into industrial control systems, including PLCs and SCADA environments, and explain how these hidden risks affect sectors such as chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and automotive manufacturing. You will also apply practical strategies to recognise and assess AI-related threats within control layers, helping you protect critical industrial processes across a range of manufacturing settings.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Industrial Control Systems: Hidden Risks
lessons.Description: As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly embedded in industrial environments, it introduces a new layer of risk that many engineers and operators are only beginning to understand. This lesson explores how AI integration within programmable logic controllers (PLCs), SCADA systems, and broader control architectures can create vulnerabilities that traditional security approaches were never designed to address. We will examine real-world scenarios where AI-driven automation has expanded the attack surface, introduced unpredictable behaviours, or undermined established safety protocols. The content is designed to be accessible for those without a deep technical background, offering clear explanations and practical context throughout. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer picture of where the hidden risks lie and why they deserve serious attention in any modern industrial setting.
lessons.content: New vulnerabilities introduced in PLCs, SCADA, and control layers.

slides.subtitle: The evolution from standalone to AI-integrated infrastructure
slides.slidecontent:
• Industrial control systems transitioning from isolated to AI-enhanced operations
• PLCs, SCADA, and control layers now incorporate machine learning components
• Traditional operational technology (OT) networks expanding beyond original design
• Manufacturing sectors across chemicals, pharmaceuticals, automotive adapting rapidly
• New capabilities come with previously unknown security implications

slides.subtitle: How AI components create unexpected entry points
slides.slidecontent:
• AI algorithms introduce dynamic code execution paths not present in traditional systems
• Machine learning models require continuous data feeds from multiple sensors
• Training data pipelines create new network communication channels
• AI decision-making processes operate outside established security monitoring
• Remote AI model updates bypass traditional change control procedures

slides.subtitle: When decades-old systems meet modern AI demands
slides.slidecontent:
• PLCs designed 20+ years ago lack computational resources for AI processing
• Communication protocols like Modbus lack encryption or authentication
• No security update mechanisms for aging control hardware
• Fixed memory allocations cannot accommodate AI model storage requirements
• Real-time control loops vulnerable to AI processing delays

slides.subtitle: How corrupted training data undermines control decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• Malicious actors can manipulate training datasets before AI deployment
• Gradual data corruption over time degrades AI decision accuracy
• Sensor spoofing feeds false information directly into AI algorithms
• Model drift causes AI behavior to change unpredictably over time
• No traditional signature-based detection for poisoned AI outputs

slides.subtitle: Vulnerabilities embedded before deployment
slides.slidecontent:
• AI libraries and frameworks often downloaded from public repositories
• Third-party AI components may contain backdoors or malware
• Vendor-supplied AI models trained on unknown or compromised datasets
• Update mechanisms for AI components bypass internal security reviews
• Shared development tools across vendor networks spread contamination

slides.subtitle: Exploiting AI decision-making weaknesses
slides.slidecontent:
• Small input modifications cause AI to make dramatically wrong decisions
• Attackers craft sensor inputs specifically to fool machine learning models
• AI systems lack human intuition to detect obviously wrong scenarios
• Adversarial examples transfer between different AI implementations
• Traditional security tools cannot identify these AI-specific attacks

slides.subtitle: Building defense against AI-specific threats
slides.slidecontent:
• Implement AI output validation using independent verification systems
• Create isolated AI processing zones separate from critical control functions
• Deploy anomaly detection specifically tuned for AI behavior patterns
• Establish AI model version control and rollback capabilities
• Regular adversarial testing of AI decision-making under attack scenarios

slides.subtitle: Next steps for securing AI-enhanced control systems
slides.slidecontent:
• Inventory all AI components currently deployed in your environment
• Assess data flows between AI systems and critical control functions
• Develop AI-specific incident response procedures
• Train operations staff to recognize AI-related anomalies
• Establish ongoing monitoring for AI model performance degradation

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI in Industrial Control Systems and the hidden risks that come with this technological evolution. Industrial environments across chemicals, pharmaceuticals, automotive, and other manufacturing sectors are rapidly integrating artificial intelligence into their control systems. What once were standalone PLCs and isolated SCADA networks are now becoming AI-enhanced systems that can analyze patterns, predict failures, and optimize operations in real time. This transformation brings tremendous benefits, but it also introduces security challenges that traditional operational technology was never designed to handle. Throughout this course, we'll explore these new vulnerabilities and equip you with the knowledge to identify and address AI-specific risks in industrial environments.

When we add AI capabilities to industrial control systems, we fundamentally change how these systems operate and communicate. Unlike traditional control logic that follows predictable, predetermined paths, AI algorithms create dynamic execution patterns that can vary based on the data they receive. These systems require continuous data feeds from multiple sensors and external sources, creating new network pathways that didn't exist before. Machine learning models make decisions through processes that are often opaque and difficult to monitor using conventional security tools. Additionally, the ability to update AI models remotely means that system behavior can change without going through established change control procedures, creating new opportunities for attackers to exploit.

One of the biggest challenges in securing AI-enhanced industrial systems is the fundamental mismatch between legacy infrastructure and modern AI requirements. Many PLCs controlling critical processes today were installed decades ago, when the primary concerns were reliability and uptime, not cybersecurity. These systems use communication protocols like Modbus that were designed for simplicity and reliability, not security - they lack basic features like encryption or authentication. The hardware itself often cannot support the computational demands of AI processing, leading to workarounds that introduce additional security gaps. When you try to integrate AI capabilities with these legacy systems, you create compatibility issues that can destabilize control loops that have operated reliably for years.

AI model poisoning represents a particularly insidious threat to industrial control systems because it attacks the foundation of AI decision-making: the training data. Unlike traditional malware that disrupts systems immediately, poisoned AI models can operate normally for months while gradually making increasingly poor decisions. Attackers can corrupt training datasets during the development phase, or manipulate sensor inputs to feed false information into deployed AI systems. What makes this especially dangerous is that there's no traditional signature-based detection method for identifying when an AI system has been compromised in this way. The AI continues to operate within normal parameters while making decisions based on fundamentally flawed logic.

The complexity of modern AI systems creates extensive supply chain vulnerabilities that extend far beyond traditional hardware and software components. AI implementations often rely on publicly available libraries and frameworks that may not have undergone rigorous security review. Third-party vendors may supply AI models that were trained on datasets of unknown origin or quality. The rapid pace of AI development means that security updates and patches are frequently downloaded automatically from public repositories, bypassing internal security review processes. When development tools and platforms are shared across vendor networks, a compromise at one supplier can quickly spread to multiple clients, creating cascading vulnerabilities across the industrial ecosystem.

Adversarial attacks represent a completely new category of threat that specifically exploits the way AI systems process and interpret data. These attacks work by making tiny, often imperceptible changes to sensor inputs that cause AI systems to make dramatically incorrect decisions. For example, an attacker might subtly alter temperature readings in a way that humans would never notice, but that causes an AI system to incorrectly assess equipment health or safety conditions. What makes these attacks particularly concerning is that they exploit fundamental weaknesses in how machine learning models process information, rather than traditional software vulnerabilities. Traditional security monitoring tools are not designed to detect these AI-specific attack patterns.

Protecting AI-enhanced industrial systems requires a fundamentally different approach to security that addresses the unique characteristics of artificial intelligence. The most critical strategy is implementing independent validation systems that verify AI outputs before they can affect critical control functions. This means creating architectural separation between AI processing and essential safety systems. You'll also need anomaly detection capabilities specifically designed to identify when AI systems are behaving unexpectedly, as traditional monitoring tools may not catch AI-specific problems. Version control becomes crucial - you need the ability to quickly roll back to previous AI model versions if something goes wrong. Regular adversarial testing helps you understand how your AI systems might behave under attack.

Moving forward, securing your AI-enhanced industrial systems requires a systematic approach that begins with understanding your current exposure. Start by creating a comprehensive inventory of all AI components in your environment, including third-party systems and vendor-supplied models. Map out how data flows between these AI systems and your critical control functions to identify potential attack paths. Develop specific incident response procedures for AI-related security events, because these may require different response strategies than traditional IT security incidents. Most importantly, ensure your operations staff understand how to recognize when AI systems are behaving abnormally, and establish ongoing monitoring to detect gradual degradation in AI model performance that might indicate a security compromise.

Who is this for: Plant managers, manufacturing engineers, operations managers, production supervisors, facility managers, maintenance technicians, quality assurance managers, industrial automation specialists
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, Manufacturing, Engineering, Production, Maintenance, Quality Assurance, Plant Management
